title: Mucus Production
desc: The resinous mucus seals tunnel walls. Processing it is exactly as unpleasant as it sounds.

steps.text:
Every tunnel in Mount Pillar is sealed with mucus — a resinous secretion that Danians produce naturally and process industrially. The mucus hardens into a waterproof, air-tight coating that prevents seepage, insulates against temperature swings, and gives the tunnels their characteristic smooth, slightly glossy appearance.

The processing plant is a series of mixing vats where raw mucus is blended with mineral additives to create sealant of various grades. Construction grade for new tunnels. Repair grade for patches. Royal grade for the Queen's chambers — the composition of which is a state secret.

The plant supervisor hands you a mixing paddle and points at Vat Two. "Standard construction blend. Ratio is on the board — three parts raw, one part calcium powder, half-part binding agent. Mix until it's the consistency of thick honey. Don't let it set in the vat — if it hardens before application, we have to chip it out."

options.label: Help with the application crew instead

steps.text:
Mixing is heavy work. The raw mucus is thick, translucent, and clings to everything. You add calcium powder in measured scoops, stirring continuously. The mixture resists — pushing back against the paddle like something alive.

The binding agent goes in last. The moment it hits the mixture, the consistency changes — the mucus tightens, becomes elastic, starts developing the adhesive properties that make it useful. You mix faster, incorporating the agent evenly before it can create lumps.

The supervisor checks the batch. She dips a stick, pulls it out, watches how the mucus drips. "Good viscosity. See how it stretches before it drops? That's the binding agent distributed correctly. Lumpy batches drip fast — the adhesion is uneven."

She marks the batch as approved. The vat is fitted with a spigot, and the construction crews will draw from it directly, filling application tools and heading to the new tunnels.

"This batch will seal about two hundred feet of corridor," the supervisor says. "The expansion project needs six hundred feet sealed by end of week. So we'll be mixing two more batches tomorrow."

You wash your hands. The mucus doesn't wash off easily — it's designed not to. You scrub with a solvent that smells like turpentine and eventually your hands return to their normal color.

The supervisor notices your scrubbing. "The old-timers don't bother washing. Their hands are permanently coated. Best waterproofing in Mount Pillar."

options.label: Consider the merits of permanently waterproof hands

steps.text:
The application crew works at the frontier — the raw, newly-cut tunnels where stone is exposed and unsealed. Their job is to coat every surface with construction-grade mucus before the tunnel can be occupied.

The application tool is a broad trowel — you scoop mucus from a portable pot and spread it on the walls in long, even strokes. The mucus is warm, slightly sticky, and adheres to stone on contact. It self-levels to some degree, flowing into cracks and pores, creating a seamless seal.

You start on a section of wall near Queen's Gate — a high-traffic area where the seal needs to be extra smooth. The technique is similar to plastering — even pressure, consistent thickness, overlapping strokes.

The mucus sets fast in the tunnel's ambient temperature. You have about ten minutes of working time before it begins to cure. After that, any adjustment creates visible marks. The application crew works in sections — seal a ten-foot section, let it cure, move on.

Your section cures smooth and glossy. The crew lead runs a hand over it. "Clean application. No bubbles, no thin spots." She shines a lamp at the surface. The mucus catches light, refracting it slightly, giving the tunnel wall a warm amber glow. "That's the sign of a proper seal — even refraction. If you see dark spots, the coating is thin and needs another layer."

By the end of the shift, forty feet of tunnel are sealed. The stone is protected, the corridor is waterproof, and Mount Pillar grows a little more livable.

options.label: Admire the amber glow of a proper seal
